narrative: Dear [Student's Name], I know this rejection letter stings—dreaming of law school and the bar exam ahead, only to face this setback feels deeply personal, and I'm here to walk you through why our predictive model saw your path tipping toward a 45% chance of not passing the bar, leading to this tough call on your application. Picture your journey starting with that Year 1 decile of 5, right in the middle of the pack at your school, which stood out as the biggest signal to the model that early struggles might snowball, pulling hardest toward the risk of failure. Then came your Year 3 decile of just 1, placing you at the very bottom by the end, reinforcing that downward trend and making the model worry your grades weren't building the momentum needed for bar success. Even with your upper-middle family income, which the model saw as not quite the buffer some others have, it nudged the outlook lower still. Your LSAT score of 34, solid but not standout, added to those concerns, as did your undergraduate GPA of 2.9, suggesting the academic foundation might not hold up under the bar's pressure. Your background as a Black male full-time student played into patterns the model has seen before, each tilting toward higher risk, though your gender actually pulled slightly in the positive direction. It's this whole picture together—starting middling, ending low, without the extra lifts in scores or support—that painted a story of vulnerability for the model, one where too many pieces aligned against crossing that bar exam finish line. This isn't about your potential or heart; it's the model's read on these combined signals from students who've walked similar paths. You've got time to strengthen those early grades or boost that LSAT—many turn stories like yours around. Hang in there; this is just one chapter.
